claims.claim_text: The opening paragraph frames a concrete production incident at a payments company without naming a real attributable source.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | opening sentence
claims.anchor_text: A platform engineer at a payments company watches a deployment dashboard turn red at the start of a busy business day.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A GitOps deployment can turn red even when the cluster itself is healthy, because the controller may be reconciling a repository change rather than responding to a cluster failure.
claims.rationale: This is presented as a real incident setup but no attributable company, postmortem, or dated source is provided.

claims.claim_text: The opening anecdote attributes the failure to a cleanup pull request deleting a manifest directory that Argo CD still treated as production intent.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters | incident cause clause
claims.anchor_text: a cleanup pull request removed a manifest directory that ArgoCD still treated as production intent
claims.suggested_rewrite: a repository change removed resources from the desired state in Git
claims.rationale: The operational lesson is sound, but the narrative presents a specific unsourced incident trigger as fact.

claims.claim_text: Argo CD separates responsibilities across the API server, repo server, application controller, and Redis cache.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Concept: Reconciliation Is the Product | components paragraph and component table
claims.rationale: The shared architecture source directly documents these core Argo CD components and their responsibilities.

claims.claim_text: The official getting-started flow installs Argo CD into the `argocd` namespace from the stable install manifest, and production should pin a version instead of tracking stable.
claims.span_hint: section: Installing ArgoCD Without Confusing Bootstrap and Delivery | install paragraph before first bash block
claims.rationale: The getting-started guide shows the stable-manifest install and explicitly recommends a pinned version for production.

claims.claim_text: The default install uses a self-signed certificate, supports local access via `kubectl port-forward`, and stores the generated admin password in `argocd-initial-admin-secret` until you change it.
claims.span_hint: section: Installing ArgoCD Without Confusing Bootstrap and Delivery | password and port-forward paragraphs
claims.rationale: The getting-started guide explicitly documents the self-signed default, port-forward access, the initial admin secret, and the warning to delete it after changing the password.

claims.claim_text: An Argo CD `Application` specifies project, source, destination, and sync policy, and the source can point to either a Git repository or a Helm chart repository.
claims.span_hint: section: Modeling an Application: Source, Destination, Project, Sync | opening paragraph and first YAML example
claims.rationale: The Application specification reference directly defines these fields and notes that `repoURL` may target Git or Helm sources.

claims.claim_text: Automated sync applies changes when live state differs from Git, `selfHeal` retriggers sync on live drift, and pruning controls automatic deletion of resources removed from desired state.
claims.span_hint: section: Sync Policy: Automation With a Blast Radius | opening paragraphs
claims.rationale: The automated-sync documentation directly defines auto-sync, self-heal, and automatic pruning behavior.

claims.claim_text: `PruneLast=true` makes pruning happen as a final implicit wave after other resources are deployed, healthy, and prior waves succeed.
claims.span_hint: section: Sync Policy: Automation With a Blast Radius | `PruneLast=true` paragraph
claims.rationale: The sync-options documentation explicitly defines `PruneLast=true` as a final implicit sync wave after resources become healthy.

claims.claim_text: Argo CD supports hook phases such as `PreSync`, `Sync`, `PostSync`, `SyncFail`, and `PostDelete`, and it orders sync waves from lower numbers to higher numbers.
claims.span_hint: section: Sync Policy: Automation With a Blast Radius | sync waves paragraph, hook paragraph, and hook table
claims.rationale: The sync-phases-and-waves reference directly defines the hook lifecycle and the lowest-to-highest sync-wave ordering model.

claims.claim_text: The App of Apps pattern uses a parent Application to create child Applications, and Argo CD documents it as an admin-level cluster-bootstrapping tool.
claims.span_hint: section: ApplicationSets and App of Apps: Scaling Without Copying Risk | App of Apps paragraphs
claims.rationale: The cluster-bootstrapping guide documents the App of Apps pattern and warns that it is an admin-only capability.

claims.claim_text: ApplicationSets generate `Application` resources from generators such as Git directories, lists, clusters, matrix, merge, and pull requests.
claims.span_hint: section: ApplicationSets and App of Apps: Scaling Without Copying Risk | opening paragraph, generator examples, and generator table
claims.rationale: The shared ApplicationSet source covers generator-driven creation of Application resources and the documented generator families used here.

claims.claim_text: Projects can restrict trusted source repositories, destination clusters and namespaces, object kinds, and project roles, and the default project is intentionally permissive for initial testing.
claims.span_hint: section: Projects and RBAC: The Real Multi-Tenancy Boundary | AppProject discussion and Common Mistakes row about the `default` project
claims.rationale: The projects guide directly lists these restriction types and explains that the default project initially allows any repo, cluster, and kind.

claims.claim_text: Argo CD RBAC can map SSO or OIDC groups to roles, and AppProject roles narrow what users can do inside a project.
claims.span_hint: section: Projects and RBAC: The Real Multi-Tenancy Boundary | RBAC paragraph and `argocd-rbac-cm` example
claims.rationale: The shared RBAC source directly covers group mapping, built-in roles, and project-scoped access control.

claims.claim_text: Argo CD tracks application health separately from sync status.
claims.span_hint: section: Core Concept: Reconciliation Is the Product | Degraded paragraph; section: Did You Know? | health vs sync bullet
claims.rationale: The status-badge documentation explicitly describes health status and sync status as separate application status dimensions.

claims.claim_text: Argo CD supports a targeted `ignoreDifferences` rule for `/spec/replicas`, which is the documented pattern for not syncing Deployment replica count changes.
claims.span_hint: section: Troubleshooting: From Symptom to Owner | HPA drift paragraph and ignoreDifferences YAML
claims.rationale: The sync-options documentation includes the canonical `ignoreDifferences` example for ignoring `spec.replicas` during sync.

claims.claim_text: Rollback cannot be performed against an application with automated sync enabled, and `selfHeal` causes Argo CD to retry sync after live drift recurs.
claims.span_hint: section: Troubleshooting: From Symptom to Owner | rollback paragraph
claims.rationale: The automated-sync semantics page directly states both behaviors.

further_reading.title: Argo CD Architectural Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Best primary reference for controller roles, repo rendering, sync flow, and control-plane responsibilities.

further_reading.title: Sync Phases and Waves
further_reading.why_relevant: Direct reference for hooks, ordering, and the operational blast radius of sync sequencing.

further_reading.title: Projects
further_reading.why_relevant: Most relevant follow-on reading for multi-team boundaries, the default project's risks, and AppProject policy design.

notes: No claim entries were generated for code blocks, Mermaid-style ASCII diagrams, checklist success criteria, quiz prompts, or purely pedagogical analogies. I emitted only the load-bearing Argo CD product-behavior and policy claims that would need inline support if kept in prose.
